techniques.name: Appeal to Authority
techniques.definition: Leveraging emotions by referencing authoritative figures or sources to persuade audiences. Using experts, institutions, or credible-seeming persons to lend weight to a claim.
techniques.example: Monsignor Jean-François Lantheaume, who served as first Counsellor of the Nunciature in Washington, confirmed that "Vigano said the truth. That's all."
examples: Monsignor Jean-François Lantheaume, who served as first Counsellor of the Nunciature in Washington, confirmed that "Vigano said the truth. That's all."
Monsignor Jean-François Lantheaume, who served as first Counsellor of the Nunciature in Washington, confirmed that "Vigano said the truth. That's all."
Dr. Smith, a renowned epidemiologist from Harvard, stated that the vaccine is completely safe
According to senior intelligence officials, the threat level has never been higher
Expert Dr. Jane Wilson warns of catastrophe
Renowned economist Paul Krugman explains why inflation is transitory. Nobel laureates agree

commonConfusions: Not all expert citations are propaganda; distinguish authoritative sourcing from name-dropping
propaInsight.propaInsightNote: Often overlaps with glittering generalities when the authority is idealized.

techniques.name: Appeal to Fear/Prejudice
techniques.definition: Using fear, prejudice, or other strong emotions to influence the audience. Includes dramatic descriptions, catastrophic scenarios, or implicit threats.
techniques.example: A dark, impenetrable and "irreversible" winter of persecution of the faithful by their own shepherds will fall.
examples: A dark, impenetrable and "irreversible" winter of persecution of the faithful by their own shepherds will fall.
A dark, impenetrable and "irreversible" winter of persecution of the faithful by their own shepherds will fall
Immigrants are flooding our borders. Crime rates will soar
If we don't act now, economic collapse is imminent. Millions will lose their jobs
Fear sweeps the nation as the deadly virus spreads
The coming storm will devastate our communities. Prepare for the worst

commonConfusions: Legitimate warnings vs fear-mongering; proportional vs disproportionate threat framing
propaInsight.propaInsightNote: Strong emotional arousal; often combined with loaded language.

techniques.name: Bandwagon, Reductio ad Hitlerum
techniques.nameFr: Effet de foule, Réductio ad Hitlerum
techniques.definition: Bandwagon: Suggesting that everyone supports a position so the audience should too. Reductio ad Hitlerum: Comparing something to Hitler or Nazism to discredit it.
techniques.example: "Vichy journalism," a term which now fits so much of the mainstream media. It collaborates in the same way that the Vichy government in France collaborated with the Nazis.
examples: "Vichy journalism," a term which now fits so much of the mainstream media. It collaborates in the same way that the Vichy government in France collaborated with the Nazis.
Just like Soviet propaganda, they manipulate the truth
Polls show 90% of Americans support this policy. Join the majority
no different from Goebbels' propaganda machine. They spread lies to control the masses
Real Americans believe in this. Patriots everywhere support it

commonConfusions: Bandwagon: genuine popularity vs fabricated consensus. Reductio: valid analogy vs inappropriate Nazi comparison.
propaInsight.propaInsightNote: Two distinct sub-techniques; Reductio ad Hitlerum is a subtype of smears.

techniques.name: Black-and-White Fallacy
techniques.definition: Presenting two alternatives as the only possibilities, when in fact more options exist. False dilemma or false binary.
techniques.example: Francis said these words: "Everyone is guilty for the good he could have done and did not do... If we do not oppose evil, we tacitly feed it."
examples: Francis said these words: "Everyone is guilty for the good he could have done and did not do... If we do not oppose evil, we tacitly feed it."
Francis said these words: "Everyone is guilty for the good he could have done and did not do. If we do not oppose evil, we tacitly feed it."
You are either with us or against us. There is no middle ground in this fight for freedom
Either we cut spending now or we face national bankruptcy. Those are the only two options
Choose freedom or tyranny. There are only two paths. Liberty or oppression
You either support the troops or you hate America. There is no in-between

commonConfusions: Genuine dilemmas vs false dichotomy; 'either X or Y' when Z exists
propaInsight.propaInsightNote: False dilemma; closes off middle-ground alternatives.

techniques.name: Causal Oversimplification
techniques.definition: Reducing a complex cause-effect relationship to a single cause. Oversimplifying why something happened.
techniques.example: If France had not have declared war on Germany then World War II would have never happened.
examples: If France had not have declared war on Germany then World War II would have never happened.
If France had not have declared war on Germany then World War II would have never happened
Video games cause violence. It's simple. Every mass shooter played them
Close the borders and the economy will recover overnight
Unemployment rose because taxes were too high. Cut taxes and jobs will return

commonConfusions: Single cause vs multi-factor; post hoc fallacies
propaInsight.propaInsightNote: Reduces complex causality to one factor.

techniques.name: Doubt
techniques.definition: Questioning or casting doubt on the credibility of a person, institution, or claim to undermine their position.
techniques.example: Can the same be said for the Obama Administration?
examples: Can the same be said for the Obama Administration?
Can the same be said for the Obama Administration?
But can we really trust these so-called experts? Who funds their research?
The election was stolen
Skeptics ask: can we trust her funding sources?
The so-called experts might say otherwise

commonConfusions: Legitimate skepticism vs manufactured doubt (e.g. climate denial playbook)
propaInsight.propaInsightNote: Questioning without evidence to sow confusion.

techniques.name: Exaggeration/Minimisation
techniques.definition: Exaggerating the importance or severity of something, or minimising it. Downplaying harms or inflating threats.
techniques.example: Coronavirus 'risk to the American people remains very low', Trump said.
examples: Coronavirus 'risk to the American people remains very low', Trump said.
Coronavirus 'risk to the American people remains very low', Trump said
The so-called crisis is nothing more than a minor inconvenience
This is the greatest scandal in American history
It was merely a small oversight. Hardly worth mentioning

commonConfusions: Exaggeration vs hyperbole; minimisation vs proportional response
propaInsight.propaInsightNote: Bidirectional: inflate enemy/risk or shrink own responsibility.

techniques.name: Flag-Waving
techniques.definition: Using patriotic or nationalistic symbols and appeals to evoke loyalty and emotional attachment.
techniques.example: Mueller attempts to stop the will of We the People!!! It's time to jail Mueller.
examples: Mueller attempts to stop the will of We the People!!! It's time to jail Mueller.
Mueller attempts to stop the will of We the People!!! It's time to jail Mueller
True patriots stand with our troops. Real Americans support our borders
We need to show the world American strength. Make America great again
Stand with our veterans. Support our flag. America deserves leaders who love this country

commonConfusions: Genuine patriotism vs instrumental use of symbols
propaInsight.propaInsightNote: Often with slogans; appeals to in-group pride.

techniques.name: Loaded Language
techniques.definition: Using words with strong emotional connotations to influence perception. Slanted vocabulary that triggers emotional responses.
techniques.example: Outrage as Donald Trump suggests injecting disinfectant to kill virus.
examples: Outrage as Donald Trump suggests injecting disinfectant to kill virus.
Outrage as Donald Trump suggests injecting disinfectant to kill virus
The brutal regime cracked down on protesters
Radical extremists hijacked the peaceful movement. Violent mobs destroyed our cities
The ruthless dictator crushed the uprising. Innocent civilians were slaughtered

commonConfusions: Emotive language vs loaded language (systematic slant); opinion vs manipulation
propaInsight.propaInsightNote: Core technique; vocabulary as persuasion tool.

techniques.name: Name Calling/Labeling
techniques.definition: Using negative labels or epithets to describe a person or group. Attaching pejorative terms to discredit.
techniques.example: WHO: Coronavirus emergency is 'Public Enemy Number 1'
examples: WHO: Coronavirus emergency is 'Public Enemy Number 1'
WHO: Coronavirus emergency is 'Public Enemy Number 1'
The fake news media is the enemy of the people
Every single one is a lying snake
Climate deniers are dangerous fools. These anti-science fanatics threaten our future

commonConfusions: Name-calling vs legitimate criticism; labeling vs accurate description
propaInsight.propaInsightNote: Overlaps with smears; negative epithets and stereotypes.

techniques.name: Repetition
techniques.definition: Repeating a message or phrase to reinforce it and make it seem more credible or important.
techniques.example: I still have a dream. It is a dream deeply rooted in the American dream. I have a dream that...
examples: I still have a dream. It is a dream deeply rooted in the American dream. I have a dream that...
Change we can believe in. Change we can believe in. Yes we can. Change we can believe in
Jobs, jobs, jobs. That's what we need. Jobs for Americans
Freedom. Freedom. Freedom. That's what we're fighting for

commonConfusions: Rhetorical emphasis vs manipulative repetition; mantra vs propaganda loop
propaInsight.propaInsightNote: Repetition increases perceived truth (illusory truth effect).

techniques.name: Slogans
techniques.definition: Short, memorable phrases designed to encapsulate a message and evoke emotional response. Catchy statements.
examples: "BUILD THE WALL!" Trump tweeted.
"BUILD THE WALL!" Trump tweeted
"Take back control!"
"No justice, no peace."
"Yes we can!"

commonConfusions: Slogan vs headline; memorable phrase vs propaganda slogan
propaInsight.propaInsightNote: Short, punchy; often combined with flag-waving and repetition.

techniques.name: Thought-terminating Cliches
techniques.definition: Phrases that stop critical thinking. Short, stock phrases that discourage further examination.
techniques.example: I do not really see any problems there. Marx is the President.
examples: I do not really see any problems there. Marx is the President.
It is what it is
That's just common sense. Any reasonable person would agree
That's the way it's always been. Tradition matters

commonConfusions: Wise aphorism vs thought-terminator; common sense vs cliché
propaInsight.propaInsightNote: Phrases that end reflection; 'it is what it is', 'common sense'.

techniques.name: Whataboutism, Straw Men, Red Herring
techniques.nameFr: Whataboutism, homme de paille, fausse piste
techniques.definition: Whataboutism: Deflecting criticism by pointing to others' faults. Straw man: Misrepresenting an opponent's argument. Red herring: Introducing irrelevant information to distract.
techniques.example: President Trump —who himself avoided national military service in the 1960's— keeps beating the war drums over North Korea.
examples: President Trump —who himself avoided national military service in the 1960's— keeps beating the war drums over North Korea.
President Trump —who himself avoided national military service in the 1960's— keeps beating the war drums over North Korea
But what about the opposition? They take money from foreign donors too
So you want to defund the police? That means you want chaos in our streets
But look at what their side did! The other party is far worse

commonConfusions: Whataboutism vs relevant comparison; straw man vs fair paraphrase; red herring vs tangent
propaInsight.propaInsightNote: Three related techniques: deflection, misrepresentation, distraction.
